selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a busy fitness trainer in New York, I'm all too familiar with the flood of information online. When I scroll through my feeds, seeing manipulated images or fake health claims can be overwhelming. My creative side helps me think outside the box; maybe that's why knitting and baking come to mind when discussing content curation. I've learned to approach this like a craft project—selecting only the most authentic posts while ignoring the noise. For example, last week someone shared an altered photo claiming they lost weight with just lemon drops, but from my understanding of science-based fitness principles, that's impossible without diet changes. Since I'm married with two kids and juggle part-time work, time management on social media is crucial; I can only dedicate a few hours monthly to monitoring it properly.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First, acknowledge the prevalence of fake content. Then, draw from personal experience as someone who deals with misinformation regularly in their professional field. Use psychological traits like creativity and moderate agreeableness to frame content filtering as an enjoyable puzzle rather than tedious work. Apply low extraversion by suggesting introspective strategies that don't require constant engagement. Finally, incorporate high neuroticism but balance it with practical coping methods.",
  "tone": "Reflective yet pragmatic",
  "citations": ["Personal experience in health and fitness industry"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a busy fitness trainer in New York, I'm all too familiar with the flood of information online. When I scroll through my feeds, seeing manipulated images or fake health claims can be overwhelming. My creative side helps me think outside the box; maybe that's why knitting and baking come to mind when discussing content curation. I've learned to approach this like a craft project—selecting only the most authentic posts while ignoring the noise. For example, last week someone shared an altered photo claiming they lost weight with just lemon drops, but from my understanding of science-based fitness principles, that's impossible without diet changes. Since I'm married with two kids and juggle part-time work, time management on social media is crucial; I can only dedicate a few hours monthly to monitoring it properly.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, acknowledge the prevalence of fake content. Then, draw from personal experience as someone who deals with misinformation regularly in their professional field. Use psychological traits like creativity and moderate agreeableness to frame content filtering as an enjoyable puzzle rather than tedious work. Apply low extraversion by suggesting introspective strategies that don't require constant engagement. Finally, incorporate high neuroticism but balance it with practical coping methods.
citations: Personal experience in health and fitness industry
